questions.question: Explain the difference between a Packet Filtering Firewall and a Stateful Inspection Firewall.
keyPoints: Packet filtering operates at Layer 3, examines individual packets without connection context
Stateful inspection tracks active connections in a state table
Stateful provides better security by understanding connection state
Stateful can recognize if a packet belongs to an established session

questions.question: What is a DDoS attack? How does it differ from a DoS attack?
keyPoints: DDoS uses multiple compromised systems (botnet) to attack target
DoS typically uses single source to flood target
DDoS is harder to mitigate due to distributed nature
DDoS attacks are more volumetric and can overwhelm larger systems

questions.question: Describe the concept of DMZ in network security architecture.
keyPoints: DMZ is abuffer zone between internal private network and external untrusted network
Hosts public-facing services (web server, email server) in DMZ
Provides additional layer of security - if DMZ compromised, internal network still protected
Typically implemented with two firewalls - one between internet and DMZ, another between DMZ and internal network

questions.question: What is ARP Spoofing? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: ARP Spoofing sends falsified ARP messages to link attacker MAC with legitimate IP
Enables attacker to intercept, modify, or block traffic
Prevention methods include static ARP entries, ARP spoofing detection software
Switch port security and VLAN segmentation also help mitigate attacks

questions.question: Explain the working of a Proxy Firewall and list its advantages.
keyPoints: Proxy firewall acts as intermediary, terminates client connections and re-establishes to servers
Provides deep packet inspection at application layer
Hides internal network structure from external attackers
Advantages: strong security, content filtering, user authentication, logging

questions.question: What are the key features of a Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)?
keyPoints: Application-level inspection and control beyond port/protocol
User-based identity integration for policy enforcement
Integrated Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)
SSL/SSH decryption and inspection
Advanced threat protection and geolocation filtering

questions.question: Explain DNS Poisoning attack and its consequences.
keyPoints: DNS Poisoning corrupts DNS cache with false records
Redirects users from legitimate websites to malicious ones
Can lead to phishing attacks, malware installation, data theft
Prevention: DNSSEC, DNS validation, using trusted DNS servers

questions.question: Describe different types of network attacks and explain how firewalls help in preventing them. Give examples of each attack type.
keyPoints: DoS/DDoS: Flooding attacks that overwhelm systems - firewall can rate limit and filter malicious traffic
MITM: Intercepts communication - proxy firewalls can prevent by terminating connections
ARP Spoofing: Falsifies MAC-IP mapping - network segmentation and monitoring helps
Port Scanning: Reconnaissance technique - firewall can detect and block scanning attempts
SQL Injection: Targets application layer - WAF (Web Application Firewall) provides protection
Firewall types provide layered defense at different network levels
Explain how each attack bypasses security and how corresponding firewall mitigates

questions.question: Design a secure network architecture for an organization with the following requirements: (a) Internal employees need access to internal resources (b) Organization has a web server that must be accessible from internet (c) Email server should be accessible externally (d) Maximum security for internal database servers. Explain the placement of firewalls and security zones.
keyPoints: Use DMZ architecture with two firewalls
Place web server in DMZ - accessible from internet, protected by external firewall
Email server in DMZ or between firewalls for external access
Internal network with database servers behind internal firewall
Three-zone design: External (untrusted), DMZ (semi-trusted), Internal (trusted)
Firewall rules: Allow HTTP/HTTPS from internet to web server in DMZ
Allow SMTP from external to email server in DMZ
Allow internal users full access to internal resources
Deny all direct traffic from external to internal network
Use VPN for secure remote employee access

questions.question: Compare and contrast IDS and IPS. Explain how they work together with firewalls to provide comprehensive network security.
keyPoints: IDS monitors and alerts but doesn't block - passive monitoring
IPS actively blocks detected threats in real-time
Both analyze network traffic for signatures and anomalies
Firewall provides perimeter defense, IDS/IPS provides internal monitoring
IDS placement: Span port on switch, or inline for IPS
Works in layers: Firewall (first line) → IPS (inline inspection) → IDS (internal monitoring)
Correlation of alerts from all systems provides complete visibility
Example: Firewall blocks unauthorized port, IPS detects anomaly pattern, IDS logs for forensics

questions.question: Explain the principle of Least Privilege as applied to firewall rule configuration. Design a sample firewall policy for a corporate network explaining the rule hierarchy and order.
keyPoints: Least Privilege: Grant minimum access necessary for users/systems to function
Default deny approach - block all unless explicitly allowed
Rule ordering matters - first match wins in most firewalls

Sample hierarchy: 1) Block known malicious IPs/ports, 2) Allow established connections (stateful), 3) Allow specific services (HTTP 80, HTTPS 443), 4) Allow management access (restricted IPs), 5) Implicit deny all at end

Example rules: Allow outbound HTTP/HTTPS from internal to any, Allow DNS to internal DNS server, Allow SSH to internal from management network only, Deny all inbound except established connections, Log all denied traffic for analysis
Regular review and audit of rules required
